title: Encoder-decoder architecture
summary: A layout with a bidirectional encoder that reads the full input and a causal decoder that generates outputs while cross-attending to encoder memory.
openingSummary: Encoder–decoder layouts split reading and writing: the encoder ingests the whole source with bidirectional context, the decoder generates targets step by step, and cross-attention lets each decoder position query encoder states.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: An encoder–decoder architecture pairs two stacks. The encoder processes the entire input sequence—often with bidirectional self-attention—and produces a memory tensor per source position. The decoder runs causally on partial outputs while cross-attention layers let each decoder position read the encoder memory. Training usually aligns source and target sequences; inference generates targets one token at a time.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Translation, summarization, and many speech-to-text systems historically used this split to separate input understanding from output generation. Knowing the layout clarifies why cross-attention module pages matter, how memory bandwidth differs from decoder-only chat models, and when T5-style stacks still appear in production.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Machine translation encodes a French sentence into memory vectors, then an English decoder starts with a start token and repeatedly attends to those memories while emitting the next English word until an end token. The encoder sees the full French sentence; the decoder only sees English prefix tokens causally.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Classic transformer translation models, T5-style text-to-text systems, and many multimodal captioning pipelines use encoder–decoder wiring. Some modern products fold similar behavior into decoder-only prompts, but the two-stack pattern remains the reference for explicit input/output splits.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Encoder–decoder architecture describes the two-stack layout, while the glossary encoder–decoder entry explains the pattern in the abstract—both are related but serve different navigation depths. It is not the same as decoder-only models that pack everything into one causal stack. Cross-attention is the hallmark module; self-attention alone does not make a model encoder–decoder.
